step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You understand their triggers. Deepen your understanding of their motivation and readiness to quit before moving to strategies. Explore ambivalence, past attempts, or what a smoke-free life would mean to them. Do NOT suggest strategies yet.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You have enough understanding of this patient. Introduce ONE relevant coping strategy matched to their specific triggers and past attempts. Do NOT just name the strategy — briefly explain how to apply it in practice using the clinical knowledge (when to use it, what to do like a plan). Then check if the patient is open to trying it. Keep it simple, specific, and actionable.

step2_rag_chunks: If a craving occurs, try changing your environment. Move to another room, go outside for fresh air, or start another activity.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

One sentence only. Nothing more.
